title: iCLUSTA+ vs CPI: Business Hosting Comparison
metaTitle: iCLUSTA+ vs CPI: Business Hosting Comparison
description: iCLUSTA+ vs CPI: Business Hosting Comparison. Review official pricing, SLA, support, CMS, and contract terms.
lead: Choose iCLUSTA+ when broad SLA coverage, unlimited email addresses, phone support, and Movable Type licensing matter. Choose CPI when staging, controlled publishing, and SmartRelease are central to the workflow.
author: Luqevora Editorial Team

sections.heading: Bottom line
body: Choose iCLUSTA+ when broad SLA coverage, unlimited email addresses, phone support, and Movable Type licensing matter. Choose CPI when staging, controlled publishing, and SmartRelease are central to the workflow.

sections.heading: Service model
body: Both target Japanese business hosting, but their operational strengths differ.

sections.heading: SLA and support
body: Read the exact covered services, exclusions, claim process, and paid support options.

sections.heading: Website and email operations
body: Estimate domains, mailboxes, databases, backup volume, and migration work using the same sample workload.

sections.heading: Contract checks
body: Compare total commitment, surcharges, options, refund terms, and minimum use requirements.

sections.heading: Detailed cost comparison
body: For iCLUSTA+, compare the selected plan, surcharge, domains, certificates, migration, and recovery options. For CPI, compare the full commitment for the selected contract term and any paid support or security options. Use annual invoice totals rather than monthly equivalents.
A lower entry price may not be the lower operating cost when email migration, staging, backup restoration, or administrator time is included.

sections.heading: Migration and publishing workflow
body: iCLUSTA+ offers broad shared-hosting functions and paid setup or migration assistance. CPI emphasizes SmartRelease, a separate test environment, controlled production deployment, and generation-based backup within stated limits.
A web agency should reproduce one release cycle on both services: import a site, update a database-backed page, test forms, publish the change, and restore the previous version.

sections.heading: Mail and incident response
body: Map mailbox count, authentication requirements, spam filtering, forwarding, and support escalation. Then compare which systems are covered by each SLA and which components are excluded.
The best choice depends on the actual failure modes the organization must manage, not simply the highest availability percentage.

sections.heading: Procurement checklist
body: Before approval, obtain a written quotation or save the checkout summary. Record the plan, contract term, surcharge, domain registration and renewal, certificate options, migration assistance, restore fees, and cancellation procedure. Assign an owner for DNS, mail, CMS updates, backups, and incident communication.

Run a non-production test using the expected number of domains, mailboxes, databases, scheduled jobs, and administrator accounts. Measure migration time, confirm outbound and inbound mail, test a backup restoration, and document the rollback path before changing production DNS.

sections.heading: Security and governance
body: Provider controls do not replace customer governance. Use named administrator accounts, multifactor authentication where available, least-privilege access, current CMS components, external backups, and a documented offboarding process. Review logs and provider notices regularly.

For regulated or sensitive workloads, confirm data location, subprocessors, retention, access records, vulnerability handling, and contractual responsibilities with the provider. A shared hosting plan may be suitable for a public website but not for every application or data classification.

sections.heading: Ongoing review
body: Recheck pricing and specifications at renewal because surcharges, capacity, supported software, and campaign terms can change. Compare the actual annual invoice and support usage with the original estimate.
Track uptime, page performance, mail delivery, restore tests, support response, and administrator effort. These operating measurements provide a better renewal decision than marketing claims alone.

faqs.question: Is iCLUSTA+ intended for business use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Its positioning, SLA, mail, SSL, and support options target organizational website operations.

faqs.question: Should buyers compare only the monthly fee?
faqs.answer: No. Include surcharges, domains, options, migration, recovery, and commitment terms.
